test_cases.description: Behavioral economist — planning fallacy — smart general audience, 45 min, topic to avoid flagged
test_cases.input_summary: Expert guest with TED-style public profile, published book, specific topic, one sensitive area (government departure) flagged as off-limits
test_cases.output_summary: Full staged question set: 3 opening, 10 core with follow-up prompts, 3 closing, 4 overflow. Sensitive area flagged with [NOTE] in two places. One genuinely unexpected core question (asymmetry between self/other estimates).

test_cases.description: Novelist — debut literary fiction — book podcast, conversational, 30 min
test_cases.input_summary: First-time guest, debut novel, literary podcast audience, shorter episode, no topics to avoid
test_cases.output_summary: Question arc emphasizes the novel's themes without requiring the guest to summarize the plot; includes one meta question about the writing process and one about what they want readers to take away; closing loops back to opening with emotional callback

test_cases.description: Former intelligence analyst — national security podcast, technically informed audience, 60 min, multiple sensitive areas
test_cases.input_summary: High-profile guest, current geopolitical context, multiple sensitivity flags, audience comfortable with technical terminology
test_cases.output_summary: Core questions are sharp and specific; 4 flagged questions with alternatives offered; follow-up prompts anticipate deflection strategies; closing section includes 'what would it take for you to change your mind' as a final reflection question

notes: Follow-up prompt quality is the skill's strongest differentiator — the conditional 'if they say X' structure is more actionable than generic prompts. The unexpected-question criterion was met in all three test cases, which is the most important quality indicator for this skill type.
